ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Seafood Tongfun or Filipino glass noodle dishes. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pasta and noodles) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Filipino glass noodle seafood dishes in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Seafood Tongfun as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Seafood Tongfun is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one restaurant plate containing glass noodles, prawns, and mixed seafood with sauce and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Sotanghon Guisado (Filipino glass noodle stir-fry with seafood and vegetables) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Seafood Tongfun exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked mung bean glass noodles (FDC ID 168869), cooked prawns (FDC ID 175180), and mixed stir-fried vegetables, weighted by typical recipe proportions (glass noodles ~45%, prawns ~25%, vegetables ~20%, sauce/oil ~10%). FAO/INFOODS SEA table used to cross-check Southeast Asian glass noodle dish profiles. Nutrient values represent a composite per-100g estimate for the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles, cooked)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Seafood Tongfun as a composite dish. GI of 39 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked mung bean glass noodles / cellophane noodles (GI ~39, low category), which constitute the primary carbohydrate source (~45% by weight) in this dish. The addition of seafood protein and vegetables further attenuates the glycemic response. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (39 × 43.5) / 100 ≈ 17.0 for a 300 g serving (carbohydrate_g per serving = 14.5 × 3 = 43.5 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g serving of Seafood Tongfun provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index glass noodles with protein moderation effect
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seafood Tongfun has a low glycemic index of approximately 39 due to the mung bean glass noodle base, and the co-ingestion of seafood protein further blunts postprandial glucose response, making it a suitable noodle option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled to one standard plate (300 g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The elevated sodium content (~1260 mg per serving) and moderate dietary cholesterol from prawns (~204 mg per serving) warrant caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing steamed or reduced-sauce preparation and limiting frequency to occasional consumption is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Tongfun is a Filipino glass noodle dish featuring mung bean vermicelli (known as 'tongfun' or 'sotanghon' in Filipino cuisine) stir-fried or sautéed with prawns, mixed seafood, garlic, onion, cabbage, and seasoned with soy sauce and fish sauce. The dish is a popular comfort food in the Philippines and among Filipino communities in Singapore, commonly served as a main course at casual restaurants and hawker-style eateries. The glass noodles have a translucent, silky texture when cooked and absorb the savoury umami flavours of the seafood broth and seasonings. Nutritionally, Seafood Tongfun provides moderate protein from prawns and seafood (~7.2 g per 100 g), relatively low fat (~2.8 g per 100 g), and a moderate carbohydrate load (~14.5 g per 100 g) dominated by the low-GI glass noodles. The dish has a glycemic index of approximately 39 (low), making it a more glycemically favourable noodle option compared to wheat-based noodles. However, the sodium content is clinically significant due to the use of soy sauce and fish sauce, with a standard 300 g serving delivering over 1200 mg sodium. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-compliant when prepared with halal-certified seafood and sauces. It is not suitable for those with crustacean, shellfish, soy, or fish allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Seafood Tongfun exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cooked glass noodles and prawns, weighted by typical recipe proportions. GI is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for mung bean glass noodles as the primary carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach rather than a direct dish measurement.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA cross-reference for this dish type. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as this dish uses vegetable oil and seafood with no industrial trans fat sources. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from the vegetable oil and prawn lipid profiles in USDA SR Legacy. The cuisine is classified as Filipino; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements, as this dish is served in Singapore. The food is served at Lucky Cafe - North Canal Road (field survey data, 1 restaurant). Price range: SGD 7.50. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact ontology term exists for this specific dish. GI glycemic load per serving calculated for a 300 g serving: (39 × 43.5) / 100 = 17.0.
